primitives.name: Simple Harmonic Motion
primitives.formalStatement: Simple harmonic motion (SHM) is periodic motion where the restoring force is proportional to displacement: F = -kx. The solution is x(t) = A*cos(omega*t + phi) where omega = sqrt(k/m).
primitives.computationalForm: x(t) = A*cos(omega*t + phi), v(t) = -A*omega*sin(omega*t + phi), a(t) = -A*omega^2*cos(omega*t + phi). Period T = 2*pi/omega.

dependencies.description: SHM position is described by sinusoidal functions
SHM uses cosine for position when starting from maximum displacement
Amplitude determines the maximum displacement

compositionRules.yields: Complete SHM description with temporal period and spatial amplitude
conditions: When describing the full time evolution of an oscillating system
compositionRules.example: A mass on a spring with k=4 N/m, m=1 kg: omega=2 rad/s, T=pi s

applicabilityPatterns: modeling back-and-forth motion of a pendulum or spring
any system with a linear restoring force proportional to displacement

primitives.name: Frequency
primitives.formalStatement: The frequency f of a periodic phenomenon is the number of complete cycles per unit time. f = 1/T where T is the period. Measured in hertz (Hz = cycles/second).
primitives.computationalForm: f = 1/T = omega/(2*pi). omega = 2*pi*f. T = 1/f. For waves: f = v/lambda where v is wave speed and lambda is wavelength.

dependencies.description: Frequency characterizes the rate of repetition of SHM

compositionRules.yields: Wave speed: v = f * lambda, connecting temporal and spatial periodicity
conditions: When relating how fast a wave travels to its frequency and size
compositionRules.example: Sound at 440 Hz with wavelength 0.773 m travels at v = 440 * 0.773 = 340 m/s

applicabilityPatterns: determining how many oscillations occur per second
relating pitch of a sound to its physical frequency

primitives.name: Period
primitives.formalStatement: The period T of a periodic function f is the smallest positive value such that f(t + T) = f(t) for all t. T is the duration of one complete cycle.
primitives.computationalForm: T = 1/f = 2*pi/omega. For SHM with spring constant k and mass m: T = 2*pi*sqrt(m/k).

dependencies.description: Period is the reciprocal of frequency
Period generalizes the concept of periodicity from trigonometric functions

compositionRules.yields: Complete temporal characterization: T = 1/f, f = 1/T
conditions: When switching between time-per-cycle and cycles-per-time descriptions
compositionRules.example: A guitar string vibrating at 440 Hz has period T = 1/440 = 2.27 ms

applicabilityPatterns: measuring the time for one complete oscillation cycle
determining how long before a periodic system returns to its initial state

primitives.name: Angular Frequency
primitives.formalStatement: Angular frequency omega = 2*pi*f = 2*pi/T measures the rate of angular change in radians per unit time. It is the natural frequency parameter for sinusoidal functions.
primitives.computationalForm: omega = 2*pi*f = 2*pi/T. For SHM: omega = sqrt(k/m). y(t) = A*sin(omega*t + phi).

dependencies.description: Angular frequency is 2*pi times ordinary frequency
Angular frequency is measured in radians per second

compositionRules.yields: Natural sinusoidal parameterization: sin(omega*t) cycles at frequency f = omega/(2*pi)
conditions: When converting between angular and ordinary frequency
compositionRules.example: A 60 Hz signal has omega = 2*pi*60 = 377 rad/s

applicabilityPatterns: expressing the rate of rotation or oscillation in radians per second
simplifying equations of motion by eliminating factors of 2*pi

primitives.name: Wave Function
primitives.formalStatement: The general sinusoidal wave function is y(x,t) = A*sin(kx - omega*t + phi), describing a traveling wave with amplitude A, wave number k, angular frequency omega, and phase offset phi.
primitives.computationalForm: y(x,t) = A*sin(kx - omega*t + phi). Phase velocity: v = omega/k = lambda*f. At fixed x: oscillation in time. At fixed t: snapshot of spatial wave.

dependencies.description: Wave function is built on the sine function
Temporal component uses angular frequency
Amplitude scales the wave
Phase determines the initial offset

compositionRules.yields: Complete space-time wave description: y(x,t) = A*sin(kx - omega*t)
conditions: When modeling a wave propagating through space
compositionRules.example: An electromagnetic wave: E(x,t) = E0*sin(kx - omega*t) traveling at speed c = omega/k

applicabilityPatterns: describing a sinusoidal disturbance propagating through a medium
modeling light, sound, or any traveling periodic signal

primitives.name: Wavelength
primitives.formalStatement: The wavelength lambda is the spatial period of a wave: the distance over which the wave shape repeats. lambda = v/f = 2*pi/k where v is phase velocity, f is frequency, and k is wave number.
primitives.computationalForm: lambda = v/f = 2*pi/k = v*T. For light: lambda = c/f. For sound in air: lambda ~ 340/f meters.

dependencies.description: Wavelength is the spatial period of the wave function
Wavelength relates to frequency via wave speed

compositionRules.yields: Wave speed relation: v = lambda * f
conditions: When computing the speed of wave propagation
compositionRules.example: Radio waves at 100 MHz: lambda = 3e8/1e8 = 3 meters

applicabilityPatterns: measuring the spatial extent of one cycle of a wave
determining the physical size of a wave from its frequency

primitives.name: Sum-to-Product Formulas
primitives.formalStatement: sin(A) + sin(B) = 2*sin((A+B)/2)*cos((A-B)/2). cos(A) + cos(B) = 2*cos((A+B)/2)*cos((A-B)/2). These convert sums of sinusoids into products.
primitives.computationalForm: sin(A)+sin(B) = 2*sin((A+B)/2)*cos((A-B)/2). cos(A)+cos(B) = 2*cos((A+B)/2)*cos((A-B)/2). sin(A)-sin(B) = 2*cos((A+B)/2)*sin((A-B)/2).

dependencies.description: Formulas involve sine
Formulas involve cosine
Derivable from addition formulas which relate to double angle identities

compositionRules.yields: Analysis of combined waves: sum of two sinusoids reveals beat and carrier frequencies
conditions: When two waves of nearly equal frequency are added
compositionRules.example: sin(440*t) + sin(442*t) = 2*cos(t)*sin(441*t), producing beats at 2 Hz

applicabilityPatterns: analyzing the combination of two sinusoidal signals at different frequencies
detecting beat frequencies from the superposition of close-frequency waves

primitives.name: Product-to-Sum Formulas
primitives.formalStatement: sin(A)*sin(B) = [cos(A-B) - cos(A+B)]/2. cos(A)*cos(B) = [cos(A-B) + cos(A+B)]/2. sin(A)*cos(B) = [sin(A+B) + sin(A-B)]/2.
primitives.computationalForm: sin(A)*cos(B) = [sin(A+B) + sin(A-B)]/2. Used to simplify products of trig functions into sums, especially for integration.

dependencies.description: Product-to-sum formulas are the algebraic inverses of sum-to-product formulas

compositionRules.yields: Complete toolkit for converting between product and sum forms of trigonometric expressions
conditions: When simplifying trigonometric expressions for integration or analysis
compositionRules.example: integral sin(3x)*cos(5x) dx = integral [sin(8x) + sin(-2x)]/2 dx

applicabilityPatterns: simplifying products of trigonometric functions for integration
analyzing amplitude modulation as sum of two frequencies

primitives.name: Superposition Principle
primitives.formalStatement: For linear systems, the net response at a given point caused by two or more stimuli is the sum of the responses that would have been caused by each stimulus individually. For waves: y_total(x,t) = y_1(x,t) + y_2(x,t) + ...
primitives.computationalForm: y_total = sum_i y_i. If y_i = A_i*sin(k_i*x - omega_i*t + phi_i), then y_total = sum A_i*sin(k_i*x - omega_i*t + phi_i). Holds for linear wave equations.

dependencies.description: Superposition applies to wave functions

compositionRules.yields: Complete interference analysis: constructive when in-phase, destructive when out-of-phase
conditions: When multiple waves interact at the same point in space
compositionRules.example: Two speakers playing the same note: loud spots (constructive) and quiet spots (destructive)

applicabilityPatterns: adding together multiple wave sources to find the combined effect
analyzing interference patterns from multiple coherent sources

primitives.name: Phasor Representation
primitives.formalStatement: A sinusoidal signal A*cos(omega*t + phi) is represented as a complex number (phasor) A*e^(i*phi) in the complex plane. Phasor addition corresponds to vector addition of rotating vectors, simplifying the analysis of combined sinusoids.
primitives.computationalForm: Phasor: P = A*e^(i*phi). Signal: Re(P*e^(i*omega*t)) = A*cos(omega*t + phi). Sum: P_total = P_1 + P_2 = A_1*e^(i*phi_1) + A_2*e^(i*phi_2).

dependencies.description: Phasors are complex numbers representing sinusoidal signals
Phasor representation is based on Euler's formula
Phasors represent sinusoidal wave functions

compositionRules.yields: Adding sinusoids by vector addition of their phasors
conditions: When combining multiple sinusoidal signals at the same frequency
compositionRules.example: Two signals 3*cos(t) + 4*cos(t+pi/2): phasors 3+4i, |phasor_sum| = 5, combined signal = 5*cos(t + arctan(4/3))

applicabilityPatterns: simplifying the addition of sinusoidal signals using complex arithmetic
analyzing AC circuits where voltages and currents are sinusoidal

primitives.name: Harmonic Series
primitives.formalStatement: The harmonic series of a fundamental frequency f_1 consists of integer multiples: f_n = n * f_1 for n = 1, 2, 3, ... The nth harmonic has frequency n times the fundamental.
primitives.computationalForm: f_n = n * f_1. Wavelengths: lambda_n = lambda_1 / n. String fixed at both ends: f_n = n * v/(2L) where v is wave speed, L is string length.

dependencies.description: Harmonic series is built from multiples of the fundamental
Harmonics are indexed by natural number multiples

compositionRules.yields: Complex tones: real sounds as sums of harmonics with varying amplitudes
conditions: When analyzing the frequency content of a musical instrument's sound
compositionRules.example: A clarinet emphasizes odd harmonics: f, 3f, 5f, 7f with decreasing amplitudes

applicabilityPatterns: determining the frequency content of a vibrating string or air column
understanding why different instruments sound different even playing the same note

primitives.name: Fundamental Frequency
primitives.formalStatement: The fundamental frequency f_1 is the lowest resonant frequency of a vibrating system. For a string of length L with wave speed v: f_1 = v/(2L). All higher harmonics are integer multiples of f_1.
primitives.computationalForm: f_1 = v/(2L) for a string. f_1 = v/(4L) for a pipe closed at one end. f_1 = v/(2L) for a pipe open at both ends.

dependencies.description: Fundamental frequency is the specific lowest frequency of a resonant system
Resonant systems exhibit harmonic motion at their fundamental

compositionRules.yields: Complete spectral description of a vibrating system: f_n = n * f_1
conditions: When determining all resonant frequencies of a vibrating body
compositionRules.example: Guitar A string: f_1 = 110 Hz, harmonics at 220, 330, 440, ... Hz

applicabilityPatterns: finding the lowest pitch produced by a vibrating string or air column
tuning musical instruments to a specific pitch

primitives.name: Overtones
primitives.formalStatement: Overtones are the frequencies of vibration above the fundamental. The nth overtone is the (n+1)th harmonic: f_{overtone_n} = (n+1)*f_1. The first overtone is the second harmonic.
primitives.computationalForm: 1st overtone = 2*f_1 (octave above fundamental). 2nd overtone = 3*f_1. Relative amplitudes determine the timbre of the sound.

dependencies.description: Overtones are the harmonics above the fundamental
Overtones are defined relative to the fundamental

compositionRules.yields: Sound character: the relative strengths of overtones determine instrument identity
conditions: When analyzing or synthesizing instrument sounds
compositionRules.example: A flute has weak overtones (nearly pure tone), a violin has strong overtones (rich tone)

applicabilityPatterns: analyzing the spectral content of a musical note beyond its fundamental pitch
understanding why a note played on different instruments sounds different

primitives.name: Timbre
primitives.formalStatement: Timbre (tone color) is the quality of a sound that distinguishes different sources playing the same pitch at the same loudness. It is determined by the relative amplitudes and phases of the harmonics in the sound's spectrum.
primitives.computationalForm: Timbre is characterized by the spectral envelope: {A_n} where sound = sum A_n * sin(n*omega_1*t + phi_n). Different {A_n} profiles = different timbres.

dependencies.description: Timbre is determined by the pattern of overtones
The harmonic series provides the frequency components that determine timbre

compositionRules.yields: Mathematical representation of any periodic sound as a Fourier series, with coefficients encoding timbre
conditions: When mathematically analyzing or synthesizing sound quality
compositionRules.example: Synthesizing a violin sound by setting A_n ~ 1/n and appropriate phases

applicabilityPatterns: distinguishing between instruments playing the same note
designing sound synthesis algorithms that mimic real instruments

primitives.name: Musical Interval Ratios
primitives.formalStatement: Musical intervals correspond to simple frequency ratios: octave (2:1), perfect fifth (3:2), perfect fourth (4:3), major third (5:4), minor third (6:5). Consonant intervals have small-integer ratios.
primitives.computationalForm: Given base frequency f: octave = 2f, fifth = 3f/2, fourth = 4f/3, major third = 5f/4. Equal temperament: semitone ratio = 2^(1/12) ~ 1.0595.

dependencies.description: Intervals are defined as ratios of fundamental frequencies
Musical intervals are rational number ratios

compositionRules.yields: Natural occurrence of musical intervals within the harmonic series of a single note
conditions: When explaining why certain intervals sound consonant
compositionRules.example: The 2nd and 3rd harmonics (2f, 3f) form a perfect fifth (3:2 ratio)

applicabilityPatterns: constructing musical scales from frequency ratios
understanding consonance and dissonance in music theory

primitives.name: Divergence of the Harmonic Series
primitives.formalStatement: The harmonic series sum_{n=1}^{infinity} 1/n = 1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4 + ... diverges to infinity. Despite each term approaching 0, the partial sums grow without bound.
primitives.computationalForm: Proof by grouping: 1 + 1/2 + (1/3+1/4) + (1/5+1/6+1/7+1/8) + ... >= 1 + 1/2 + 1/2 + 1/2 + ... Partial sum S_n ~ ln(n) + gamma where gamma ~ 0.5772 (Euler-Mascheroni constant).

dependencies.description: The mathematical harmonic series 1/n is named after musical harmonics
The series is indexed over natural numbers

compositionRules.yields: Integral test connection: integral of 1/x from 1 to infinity diverges, matching the harmonic series
conditions: When using the integral test for series convergence
compositionRules.example: integral_1^N 1/x dx = ln(N) grows without bound, so sum 1/n diverges

applicabilityPatterns: determining that a series with terms decreasing to zero can still diverge
estimating how many terms of the harmonic series are needed to reach a given sum

primitives.name: Resonance Conditions
primitives.formalStatement: Resonance occurs when a driving force oscillates at a natural frequency of the system. At resonance, energy transfer is maximized and amplitude reaches a peak. For a damped oscillator driven at frequency omega_d: maximum amplitude when omega_d = omega_0*sqrt(1 - 2*zeta^2).
primitives.computationalForm: Resonance frequency: omega_r = omega_0 for undamped. Amplitude at resonance: A_max = F_0 / (2*m*omega_0*zeta) for light damping. Quality factor Q = omega_0 / (2*zeta*omega_0) = 1/(2*zeta).

dependencies.description: Resonance occurs at natural frequencies which include the fundamental
Resonance is a phenomenon of driven harmonic oscillators

compositionRules.yields: Driven damped oscillator: full analysis of forced vibrations including resonance peak
conditions: When a periodic force drives a naturally oscillating system
compositionRules.example: A child pushing a swing at its natural frequency produces large amplitude oscillations

applicabilityPatterns: predicting when a driven system will achieve maximum amplitude
designing systems to either exploit or avoid resonance effects

primitives.name: Beat Frequency
primitives.formalStatement: When two sinusoidal waves of frequencies f_1 and f_2 are superposed, the result exhibits amplitude modulation at the beat frequency f_beat = |f_1 - f_2|. Using sum-to-product: sin(2*pi*f_1*t) + sin(2*pi*f_2*t) = 2*cos(2*pi*f_beat/2 * t)*sin(2*pi*f_avg * t).
primitives.computationalForm: f_beat = |f_1 - f_2|. f_average = (f_1 + f_2)/2. Perceived as a pulsating sound at rate f_beat with carrier at f_average.

dependencies.description: Beats arise from the superposition of two waves
Sum-to-product identity reveals the beat and carrier frequencies
Beat frequency is the difference of two frequencies

compositionRules.yields: Practical tuning method: beats slow down and vanish when frequencies match
conditions: When two nearly-equal frequencies combine
compositionRules.example: Tuning a guitar: 440 Hz + 442 Hz produces 2 beats per second

applicabilityPatterns: tuning musical instruments by listening for beats to disappear
measuring small frequency differences between two oscillators

primitives.name: Constructive and Destructive Interference
primitives.formalStatement: When two waves meet: constructive interference occurs when they are in phase (phase difference = 2n*pi), amplitudes add; destructive interference when out of phase (phase difference = (2n+1)*pi), amplitudes cancel.
primitives.computationalForm: Two waves A*sin(kx+phi_1) and A*sin(kx+phi_2): constructive when phi_2-phi_1 = 2n*pi (amplitude 2A), destructive when phi_2-phi_1 = (2n+1)*pi (amplitude 0). General: A_total = 2A*|cos((phi_2-phi_1)/2)|.

dependencies.description: Interference is a consequence of superposition
The type of interference depends on the phase relationship

compositionRules.yields: Standing wave patterns from constructive/destructive interference of counter-propagating waves
conditions: When identical waves travel in opposite directions in a bounded medium
compositionRules.example: A guitar string: incident and reflected waves interfere to create nodes and antinodes

applicabilityPatterns: predicting where sound will be loud or quiet in a room with reflections
designing noise-cancellation systems using destructive interference

primitives.name: Wave Equation
primitives.formalStatement: The one-dimensional wave equation is the second-order partial differential equation: d^2u/dt^2 = c^2 * d^2u/dx^2, where c is the wave propagation speed and u(x,t) is the displacement field.
primitives.computationalForm: PDE: u_tt = c^2 * u_xx. Solutions: u(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct) (D'Alembert). Sinusoidal: u = A*sin(kx - omega*t) with c = omega/k.

dependencies.description: Sinusoidal waves are particular solutions of the wave equation
The wave equation involves second partial derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Normal mode solutions: u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t) leading to standing waves
conditions: When solving the wave equation with boundary conditions
compositionRules.example: String fixed at both ends: u(x,t) = sum B_n * sin(n*pi*x/L) * cos(n*pi*c*t/L)

applicabilityPatterns: modeling wave propagation in strings, air columns, or electromagnetic fields
predicting how disturbances travel through a medium

primitives.name: Phase Velocity
primitives.formalStatement: The phase velocity v_p of a wave is the speed at which a point of constant phase travels. For a wave y = A*sin(kx - omega*t): v_p = omega/k = lambda*f. In non-dispersive media, phase velocity equals group velocity.
primitives.computationalForm: v_p = omega/k = lambda*f = lambda/T. For the wave equation u_tt = c^2*u_xx: v_p = c (constant). For dispersive media: v_p = v_p(k).

dependencies.description: Phase velocity characterizes the propagation of the wave function
Phase velocity is the ratio of angular frequency to wave number

compositionRules.yields: Complete wave propagation characterization: crests travel at v_p, energy travels at v_g
conditions: When distinguishing between phase and energy transport in dispersive media
compositionRules.example: Deep water waves: v_p = sqrt(g*lambda/(2*pi)), v_g = v_p/2

applicabilityPatterns: computing the speed at which wave crests advance through a medium
determining the wavelength of a wave from its frequency and medium properties

primitives.name: Group Velocity
primitives.formalStatement: The group velocity v_g = d(omega)/dk is the speed at which the envelope (and energy) of a wave packet propagates. In non-dispersive media: v_g = v_p. In dispersive media: v_g != v_p.
primitives.computationalForm: v_g = d(omega)/dk. For omega = omega(k): v_g = v_p + k * dv_p/dk. If omega = c*k (non-dispersive): v_g = c = v_p.

dependencies.description: Group velocity generalizes phase velocity for wave packets
Group velocity is defined as the derivative of omega with respect to k

compositionRules.yields: Quantitative measure of how quickly wave packets spread: v_g != v_p implies dispersion
conditions: When wave packets propagate in a medium where speed depends on frequency
compositionRules.example: Light in glass: different colors have different phase velocities, causing prismatic dispersion

applicabilityPatterns: computing the speed at which information or energy is transported by a wave
analyzing how wave packets spread in dispersive media

primitives.name: Dispersion
primitives.formalStatement: Dispersion occurs when the phase velocity of a wave depends on its frequency or wavelength: v_p = v_p(omega). This causes different frequency components to travel at different speeds, spreading wave packets over time.
primitives.computationalForm: Dispersion relation: omega = omega(k). Non-dispersive: omega = c*k (linear). Dispersive: omega = c*k + alpha*k^3 (example). Measure: d^2(omega)/dk^2 != 0.

dependencies.description: Dispersion means phase velocity varies with frequency
Dispersion is characterized by v_g != v_p

compositionRules.yields: Modified wave equations with dispersion terms: u_tt = c^2*u_xx + beta*u_xxxx
conditions: When modeling waves in media where short wavelengths travel differently
compositionRules.example: Water waves: short ripples are dispersive (capillary), long swells are less so (gravity)

applicabilityPatterns: explaining why white light splits into colors through a prism
predicting how a pulse broadens as it travels through fiber optics

primitives.name: Wave Number
primitives.formalStatement: The wave number k = 2*pi/lambda is the spatial frequency of a wave, measuring the number of radians per unit distance. It is the spatial analog of angular frequency.
primitives.computationalForm: k = 2*pi/lambda = omega/v_p. Wave function: y = A*sin(kx - omega*t). Units: radians per meter.

dependencies.description: Wave number is the reciprocal of wavelength scaled by 2*pi
Wave number is the spatial counterpart of angular frequency

compositionRules.yields: Phase velocity: v_p = omega/k, connecting temporal and spatial wave parameters
conditions: When computing wave propagation speed from frequency and spatial parameters
compositionRules.example: A wave with omega = 100 rad/s and k = 5 rad/m has v_p = 20 m/s

applicabilityPatterns: measuring how many wave cycles fit in a given distance
expressing the spatial structure of a wave in mathematical equations

primitives.name: D'Alembert's Solution
primitives.formalStatement: The general solution to the 1D wave equation u_tt = c^2*u_xx is u(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct), where f and g are arbitrary twice-differentiable functions representing right-traveling and left-traveling waves respectively.
primitives.computationalForm: Given initial conditions u(x,0) = phi(x), u_t(x,0) = psi(x): f(s) = [phi(s) + (1/c)*integral_0^s psi(r)dr]/2, g(s) = [phi(s) - (1/c)*integral_0^s psi(r)dr]/2.

dependencies.description: D'Alembert's solution is the general solution of the wave equation

compositionRules.yields: Any wave motion = rightward wave + leftward wave, each propagating at speed c
conditions: When decomposing arbitrary wave motion into traveling components
compositionRules.example: A plucked string: initial triangular shape decomposes into two triangular waves traveling in opposite directions

applicabilityPatterns: finding the shape of a wave at any future time from its initial shape and velocity
understanding wave propagation as superposition of right and left traveling components

primitives.name: Energy Conservation in Waves
primitives.formalStatement: The total energy of a wave equals the sum of kinetic and potential energy. For a sinusoidal wave on a string: E = (1/2)*mu*omega^2*A^2*lambda where mu is linear density. Energy propagates at group velocity.
primitives.computationalForm: Energy density: u = (1/2)*mu*(du/dt)^2 + (1/2)*T*(du/dx)^2 where T is tension. Power: P = E/T = (1/2)*mu*omega^2*A^2*v. Intensity: I = P/area.

dependencies.description: Energy conservation follows from the wave equation's structure
Wave energy is analogous to SHM energy exchange between kinetic and potential

compositionRules.yields: Energy transport velocity: wave energy propagates at the group velocity, not the phase velocity
conditions: When computing power flow through a region
compositionRules.example: For deep water waves: energy travels at v_g = v_p/2, half the crest speed

applicabilityPatterns: computing the power carried by a wave through a medium
relating wave amplitude to energy and intensity

primitives.name: Numerical Wave Equation Solution
primitives.formalStatement: The wave equation can be solved numerically using the finite difference method. Discretize: u(i,n+1) = 2*u(i,n) - u(i,n-1) + r^2*(u(i+1,n) - 2*u(i,n) + u(i-1,n)) where r = c*dt/dx (Courant number).
primitives.computationalForm: Stability condition (CFL): r = c*dt/dx <= 1. Grid: x_i = i*dx, t_n = n*dt. Update: u_i^{n+1} = 2u_i^n - u_i^{n-1} + r^2(u_{i+1}^n - 2u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n). Apply boundary conditions at each step.

dependencies.description: Numerical method approximates solutions of the wave equation

compositionRules.yields: Comparison of numerical ODE/PDE methods: Euler for ODEs, finite differences for wave PDE
conditions: When numerically solving differential equations
compositionRules.example: Simulating a vibrating string by time-stepping the finite difference wave equation

applicabilityPatterns: simulating wave propagation on a computer when analytic solutions are unavailable
modeling complex wave phenomena like reflections and diffraction numerically

primitives.name: Separation of Variables for Waves
primitives.formalStatement: Separation of variables assumes the solution to a PDE is a product of functions of individual variables: u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t). Substituting into the wave equation and dividing by X*T yields two ODEs: X''/X = T''/(c^2*T) = -lambda (separation constant).
primitives.computationalForm: Assume u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t). Wave eq: X*T'' = c^2*X''*T. Divide: T''/(c^2*T) = X''/X = -k^2. Get: X'' + k^2*X = 0, T'' + c^2*k^2*T = 0. Solutions: X = sin(kx) or cos(kx), T = sin(ckt) or cos(ckt).

dependencies.description: Separation of variables is the primary technique for solving the wave equation with boundary conditions

compositionRules.yields: Normal mode solutions satisfying boundary conditions
conditions: When boundary conditions make direct D'Alembert solution difficult
compositionRules.example: String fixed at x=0 and x=L: X(0)=X(L)=0 forces k=n*pi/L, giving standing wave modes

applicabilityPatterns: solving the wave equation on a bounded domain with fixed or free boundary conditions
finding the natural vibration modes of a physical system

primitives.name: Standing Wave
primitives.formalStatement: A standing wave is a wave pattern that does not propagate through space but oscillates in place. It is formed by the superposition of two identical waves traveling in opposite directions: 2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t).
primitives.computationalForm: y(x,t) = 2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t). Nodes at kx = n*pi (x = n*lambda/2). Antinodes at kx = (n+1/2)*pi (x = (2n+1)*lambda/4). Amplitude varies as 2A*|sin(kx)|.

dependencies.description: Standing waves are formed by superposition of counter-propagating waves
Standing waves are products of spatial and temporal functions

compositionRules.yields: Complete modal description of vibrating systems: each mode is a standing wave at a specific frequency
conditions: When analyzing all possible vibration patterns of a bounded system
compositionRules.example: A guitar string's vibration is a sum of standing waves at f_1, 2f_1, 3f_1, ...

applicabilityPatterns: analyzing vibration patterns on strings, membranes, or in cavities
determining where resonant systems have maximum and minimum displacement

primitives.name: Node
primitives.formalStatement: A node is a point in a standing wave where the displacement is always zero. For standing wave y(x,t) = 2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t): nodes occur at x = n*lambda/2 where n = 0, 1, 2, ...
primitives.computationalForm: Node positions: x_n = n*lambda/2 = n*pi/k. For string fixed at both ends (length L): x_n = n*L/N for Nth harmonic, giving N+1 nodes (including endpoints).

dependencies.description: Nodes are a feature of standing waves

compositionRules.yields: Complete spatial amplitude profile: alternating nodes (zero) and antinodes (maximum)
conditions: When mapping the displacement pattern of a standing wave
compositionRules.example: 2nd harmonic of a string: 3 nodes (ends + center), 2 antinodes (at L/4 and 3L/4)

applicabilityPatterns: locating points of zero displacement in a resonating system
determining where to place a finger on a string to produce a harmonic

primitives.name: Antinode
primitives.formalStatement: An antinode is a point in a standing wave where the displacement amplitude is maximum. For standing wave y(x,t) = 2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t): antinodes occur at x = (2n+1)*lambda/4 where sin(kx) = +/-1.
primitives.computationalForm: Antinode positions: x_n = (2n+1)*lambda/4 = (2n+1)*pi/(2k). Maximum amplitude at antinodes is 2A. Located midway between adjacent nodes.

dependencies.description: Antinodes are a feature of standing waves
Antinodes are located midway between nodes

compositionRules.yields: Complete standing wave spatial structure: nodes and antinodes alternating at lambda/4 intervals
conditions: When describing the full spatial pattern of a standing wave
compositionRules.example: A pipe open at both ends: antinodes at open ends, nodes evenly spaced between

applicabilityPatterns: finding points of maximum vibration in a resonating system
designing acoustic spaces to place or avoid maximum sound pressure points

primitives.name: Normal Modes
primitives.formalStatement: Normal modes are the independent patterns of vibration of a system. Each mode vibrates at a single frequency (eigenfrequency) with a fixed spatial pattern (eigenfunction). Any vibration is a superposition of normal modes.
primitives.computationalForm: For string of length L: mode n has frequency f_n = n*v/(2L) and shape X_n(x) = sin(n*pi*x/L). General solution: u(x,t) = sum B_n*sin(n*pi*x/L)*cos(n*pi*v*t/L + phi_n).

dependencies.description: Each normal mode is a standing wave pattern
Normal modes arise from separation of variables in the wave equation

compositionRules.yields: Any vibration = sum of normal modes: the principle that allows Fourier decomposition of arbitrary vibrations
conditions: When representing arbitrary motion as a sum of fundamental vibration patterns
compositionRules.example: A plucked guitar string: initial triangular shape = sum of sinusoidal modes with amplitudes B_n

applicabilityPatterns: decomposing complex vibrations into independent frequency components
analyzing structural vibration by finding natural frequencies and mode shapes

primitives.name: Fourier Series (Preview)
primitives.formalStatement: Any periodic function f(x) with period L can be represented as a sum of sinusoids: f(x) = a_0/2 + sum_{n=1}^{infinity} [a_n*cos(2*pi*n*x/L) + b_n*sin(2*pi*n*x/L)] where a_n and b_n are Fourier coefficients.
primitives.computationalForm: a_n = (2/L)*integral_0^L f(x)*cos(2*pi*n*x/L) dx. b_n = (2/L)*integral_0^L f(x)*sin(2*pi*n*x/L) dx. a_0/2 = average value of f.

dependencies.description: Fourier series decomposes functions into normal mode components
Fourier series uses sine functions as basis
Fourier series uses cosine functions as basis

compositionRules.yields: Fourier series as orthogonal decomposition: sin and cos form an orthogonal basis for periodic functions
conditions: When expressing a periodic function in terms of its frequency components
compositionRules.example: Square wave: f(x) = (4/pi)*[sin(x) + sin(3x)/3 + sin(5x)/5 + ...]

applicabilityPatterns: decomposing any periodic signal into a sum of pure frequency components
analyzing the harmonic content of a complex waveform

primitives.name: Standing Wave Formation Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: The superposition of two identical sinusoidal waves traveling in opposite directions produces a standing wave: A*sin(kx - omega*t) + A*sin(kx + omega*t) = 2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t).
primitives.computationalForm: A*sin(kx-wt) + A*sin(kx+wt) = 2A*sin(kx)*cos(wt). Result: space and time are separated. Spatial envelope sin(kx) is fixed; temporal oscillation cos(wt) is uniform.

dependencies.description: Standing waves are formed by superposition
Sum-to-product identity shows the product form of two counter-propagating waves

compositionRules.yields: Connection between traveling and standing wave descriptions of bounded wave systems
conditions: When converting between traveling wave and standing wave representations
compositionRules.example: Reflection at a fixed boundary: incident + reflected wave = standing wave

applicabilityPatterns: explaining how reflections create standing wave patterns
deriving the standing wave equation from counter-propagating traveling waves

primitives.name: Boundary Conditions Determine Allowed Modes
primitives.formalStatement: For a bounded system, boundary conditions restrict the allowed wave numbers and frequencies to discrete values. Fixed ends (Dirichlet): u(0,t) = u(L,t) = 0 forces k_n = n*pi/L. Free ends (Neumann): du/dx = 0 at ends.
primitives.computationalForm: Fixed-fixed string: k_n = n*pi/L, f_n = n*v/(2L). Open-open pipe: same frequencies. Fixed-free: k_n = (2n-1)*pi/(2L), f_n = (2n-1)*v/(4L) (odd harmonics only).

dependencies.description: Boundary conditions select which standing wave modes are allowed
Boundary conditions quantize the allowed normal modes

compositionRules.yields: Physical harmonic series: boundary conditions determine whether all or only odd harmonics are present
conditions: When determining the allowed frequencies of a vibrating system
compositionRules.example: Closed pipe: only odd harmonics (clarinet). Open pipe: all harmonics (flute).

applicabilityPatterns: determining the resonant frequencies of a bounded acoustic or mechanical system
explaining why different instrument shapes produce different harmonic content

primitives.name: Fourier Series Convergence
primitives.formalStatement: For a piecewise continuous periodic function f with piecewise continuous derivative, the Fourier series converges to f(x) at points of continuity and to [f(x+) + f(x-)]/2 at points of discontinuity (Dirichlet conditions).
primitives.computationalForm: Convergence rate: if f has k continuous derivatives, then a_n, b_n = O(1/n^(k+1)). Gibbs phenomenon: at discontinuities, partial sums overshoot by ~9% regardless of number of terms.

dependencies.description: Convergence theorem describes when and how Fourier series representations are valid

compositionRules.yields: Comparison of approximation strategies: Fourier (global, periodic) vs Taylor (local, any function)
conditions: When choosing between Fourier and Taylor representations
compositionRules.example: Square wave: Fourier converges (everywhere); Taylor fails (not analytic at jumps)

applicabilityPatterns: determining whether a Fourier series will accurately represent a given function
understanding the limitations of Fourier approximation at discontinuities

primitives.name: Modal Analysis
primitives.formalStatement: Modal analysis decomposes any vibration of a linear system into a sum of normal modes. Given initial conditions, the mode amplitudes B_n are found by projecting the initial shape onto the mode shapes using inner products: B_n = (2/L)*integral_0^L f(x)*sin(n*pi*x/L) dx.
primitives.computationalForm: B_n = (2/L)*integral_0^L u(x,0)*sin(n*pi*x/L) dx. Time response: u(x,t) = sum B_n*sin(n*pi*x/L)*cos(omega_n*t). Energy in mode n proportional to B_n^2.

dependencies.description: Modal analysis decomposes into normal modes
Mode amplitudes are found by inner product projection
Modal analysis is essentially Fourier series applied to vibrating systems

compositionRules.yields: Vibration analysis as orthogonal decomposition in the space of mode shapes
conditions: When analyzing complex vibrations in terms of independent frequency components
compositionRules.example: A bridge's vibration decomposed into its first 5 normal modes to identify dominant frequencies

applicabilityPatterns: analyzing complex vibrations by decomposing into independent frequency components
determining which normal modes contribute most energy to a given vibration

primitives.name: Amplitude Modulation
primitives.formalStatement: Amplitude modulation (AM) is the variation of a carrier wave amplitude in proportion to a message signal: y(t) = [1 + m*cos(omega_m*t)] * A*cos(omega_c*t), where m is the modulation index.
primitives.computationalForm: AM signal = A*(1+m*cos(omega_m*t))*cos(omega_c*t). Spectrum: carrier + two sidebands at omega_c +/- omega_m. Bandwidth = 2*omega_m.

dependencies.description: AM involves superposition of carrier and sidebands
Product-to-sum reveals the sideband frequencies

compositionRules.yields: Radio transmission: encoding audio on a high-frequency carrier
conditions: When transmitting information via radio waves
compositionRules.example: AM radio at 1 MHz carrier with 5 kHz audio: sidebands at 995 kHz and 1005 kHz

applicabilityPatterns: encoding information onto a carrier wave by varying its amplitude
understanding radio transmission and reception

primitives.name: Wave Intensity
primitives.formalStatement: Wave intensity I is the power per unit area perpendicular to the direction of propagation. I = P/A = (1/2)*rho*v*omega^2*A^2 for mechanical waves. I is proportional to A^2 (amplitude squared).
primitives.computationalForm: I = P/A (watts/m^2). I proportional to A^2. Inverse square law: I = P/(4*pi*r^2) for a point source. Decibels: L = 10*log10(I/I_0) dB.

dependencies.description: Intensity is derived from wave energy
Intensity depends on wave amplitude

compositionRules.yields: Practical sound measurement: relating amplitude to perceived loudness
conditions: When measuring or comparing the strength of waves
compositionRules.example: Doubling distance from a point source reduces intensity by factor 4 (inverse square law)

applicabilityPatterns: measuring the power delivered by a wave per unit area
computing sound levels in decibels

primitives.name: Reflection and Transmission
primitives.formalStatement: When a wave encounters a boundary between two media, part is reflected and part is transmitted. Reflection coefficient: R = (Z2-Z1)/(Z2+Z1), Transmission: T = 2*Z2/(Z2+Z1), where Z is impedance.
primitives.computationalForm: R = (Z2-Z1)/(Z2+Z1), T = 1+R. Energy: R^2 + (Z1/Z2)*T^2 = 1. Fixed end (Z2=inf): R=-1 (inverted). Free end (Z2=0): R=+1 (not inverted).

dependencies.description: Reflection coefficients describe how wave functions split at boundaries
Standing waves form from reflected waves

compositionRules.yields: Complete boundary analysis: reflection creates standing waves in bounded media
conditions: When a wave hits a boundary and must satisfy impedance matching
compositionRules.example: String tied to wall: Z2=infinity, R=-1, wave inverts on reflection

applicabilityPatterns: predicting how much wave energy is reflected vs transmitted at a boundary
designing impedance-matched systems to minimize reflections

primitives.name: Damping
primitives.formalStatement: Damping is the dissipation of energy from an oscillating system, causing amplitude to decrease over time. Damped oscillation: x(t) = A*e^(-gamma*t)*cos(omega_d*t + phi), where gamma is the damping coefficient.
primitives.computationalForm: x(t) = A*e^(-gamma*t)*cos(omega_d*t). omega_d = sqrt(omega_0^2 - gamma^2). Underdamped: gamma < omega_0. Critically damped: gamma = omega_0. Overdamped: gamma > omega_0.

dependencies.description: Damping modifies simple harmonic motion with exponential decay

compositionRules.yields: Realistic oscillation model: amplitude decays exponentially while oscillating
conditions: When modeling oscillations with energy loss
compositionRules.example: Car suspension: critically damped for fastest return to equilibrium without oscillation

applicabilityPatterns: modeling oscillations that lose energy over time due to friction
designing systems with appropriate damping for stability

primitives.name: Interference Pattern
primitives.formalStatement: An interference pattern is the spatial distribution of constructive and destructive interference from two or more coherent wave sources. Maxima at path difference = n*lambda, minima at (n+1/2)*lambda.
primitives.computationalForm: Two-slit: d*sin(theta) = n*lambda (maxima), d*sin(theta) = (n+1/2)*lambda (minima). Fringe spacing: Delta_y = lambda*L/d on screen at distance L.

dependencies.description: Interference patterns are composed of constructive and destructive regions
Pattern spacing depends on wavelength

compositionRules.yields: Visual demonstration of wave superposition: pattern of bright and dark fringes
conditions: When two coherent sources create overlapping wave fields
compositionRules.example: Double-slit experiment: light passing through two slits creates alternating bright/dark bands on a screen

applicabilityPatterns: predicting the pattern of constructive and destructive interference
measuring wavelength from observed fringe spacing

primitives.name: Doppler Effect
primitives.formalStatement: When a wave source and observer are in relative motion, the observed frequency differs from the emitted frequency: f_obs = f_source * (v + v_obs)/(v + v_source) where v is wave speed, signs depend on direction.
primitives.computationalForm: Approaching: f_obs > f_source (blue shift). Receding: f_obs < f_source (red shift). For v_source << v: Df/f ~ v_source/v.

dependencies.description: Doppler effect describes frequency change due to motion
The effect depends on wave propagation speed

compositionRules.yields: Frequency shift measurement for determining relative velocity
conditions: When source and observer are in relative motion
compositionRules.example: Ambulance siren: pitch rises approaching (compressed wavelength), drops receding (stretched)

applicabilityPatterns: computing the frequency shift of sound or light due to relative motion
measuring velocities of distant objects using frequency shifts

primitives.name: Diffraction
primitives.formalStatement: Diffraction is the bending and spreading of waves when they encounter an obstacle or aperture. Significant when the obstacle/aperture size is comparable to the wavelength. Single slit: first minimum at sin(theta) = lambda/a.
primitives.computationalForm: Single slit minimum: a*sin(theta) = n*lambda (n != 0). Central maximum width: 2*lambda*L/a. Intensity: I = I_0 * [sin(beta/2)/(beta/2)]^2 where beta = 2*pi*a*sin(theta)/lambda.

dependencies.description: Diffraction is a wave phenomenon
Diffraction depends on wavelength relative to obstacle size
Diffraction patterns result from superposition of wavelets

compositionRules.yields: Combined diffraction and interference: multi-slit patterns modulated by single-slit envelope
conditions: When multiple apertures produce both interference and diffraction effects
compositionRules.example: Diffraction grating: sharp interference maxima within a broad diffraction envelope

applicabilityPatterns: explaining how waves bend around obstacles and through openings
predicting the angular spread of waves passing through an aperture

primitives.name: Parseval's Theorem (Preview)
primitives.formalStatement: The total energy of a periodic signal equals the sum of energies in its Fourier components: (1/L)*integral_0^L |f(x)|^2 dx = a_0^2/4 + (1/2)*sum_{n=1}^inf (a_n^2 + b_n^2).
primitives.computationalForm: (1/L)*integral |f|^2 = sum of |c_n|^2 in complex form. This is an energy conservation law for frequency decomposition.

dependencies.description: Parseval relates the energy in time domain to frequency domain via Fourier

compositionRules.yields: Energy conservation in frequency space: total power = sum of power in each harmonic
conditions: When computing total energy from spectral components
compositionRules.example: Square wave: total power = sum of odd harmonic powers (4/pi)^2 * sum 1/n^2 for odd n

applicabilityPatterns: computing total signal energy from its Fourier coefficients
verifying that a Fourier decomposition conserves total energy

primitives.name: Quality Factor (Q Factor)
primitives.formalStatement: The quality factor Q of a resonant system is the ratio of energy stored to energy lost per cycle: Q = 2*pi * (energy stored)/(energy lost per cycle) = omega_0 / (2*gamma) = omega_0 / Delta_omega.
primitives.computationalForm: Q = omega_0/(2*gamma) = f_0/Delta_f. High Q: sharp resonance peak, slow decay. Low Q: broad peak, fast decay. Bandwidth: Delta_f = f_0/Q.

dependencies.description: Q characterizes the sharpness of resonance
Q is inversely related to damping

compositionRules.yields: Complete resonance characterization: center frequency + Q determines bandwidth and decay
conditions: When designing or analyzing resonant systems
compositionRules.example: Tuning fork: Q ~ 1000, rings for minutes. Car shock absorber: Q ~ 0.5, critically damped

applicabilityPatterns: characterizing how sharply a system resonates at its natural frequency
determining the bandwidth of a resonant filter

primitives.name: Linearity of Wave Systems
primitives.formalStatement: A system is linear if it satisfies superposition: L[a*f + b*g] = a*L[f] + b*L[g]. The wave equation is linear. Nonlinear systems (shock waves, solitons) violate superposition.
primitives.computationalForm: Test: L[f+g] = L[f] + L[g] (additivity) and L[c*f] = c*L[f] (homogeneity). Linear wave equation: u_tt = c^2*u_xx. Nonlinear: u_tt = c^2*u_xx + u^3.

dependencies.description: Linearity is the condition that makes superposition valid

compositionRules.yields: Foundation for all wave decomposition methods: linearity permits Fourier analysis
conditions: When justifying the use of Fourier methods
compositionRules.example: Linear string: any vibration = sum of normal modes. Nonlinear string: modes couple and exchange energy.

applicabilityPatterns: determining whether superposition and Fourier methods apply to a system
distinguishing linear wave phenomena from nonlinear effects

primitives.name: Coupled Oscillators
primitives.formalStatement: Coupled oscillators are two or more oscillating systems connected by a coupling force. Normal modes are patterns where all oscillators move at the same frequency. N coupled oscillators have N normal modes.
primitives.computationalForm: Two coupled pendulums: omega_1 = sqrt(g/L) (in-phase), omega_2 = sqrt(g/L + 2k/m) (anti-phase). Energy transfers between oscillators at beat frequency |omega_2 - omega_1|.

dependencies.description: Each oscillator exhibits SHM
Coupled systems have normal modes

compositionRules.yields: From discrete to continuous: N coupled oscillators -> continuous string as N -> infinity
conditions: When deriving wave equations from discrete models
compositionRules.example: Chain of N masses connected by springs: as N->infinity, becomes a vibrating string obeying the wave equation

applicabilityPatterns: modeling systems of connected oscillators like molecules or coupled pendulums
understanding how wave behavior emerges from coupled discrete elements

primitives.name: Traveling Wave vs Standing Wave
primitives.formalStatement: A traveling wave y=A*sin(kx-omega*t) transports energy through space. A standing wave y=2A*sin(kx)*cos(omega*t) oscillates in place with no net energy transport. Nodes of standing waves are stationary.
primitives.computationalForm: Traveling: phase kx-omega*t advances in time (moves right). Standing: spatial and temporal parts separated, no traveling phase. Energy flux of standing wave averages to zero.

dependencies.description: Traveling waves are described by the general wave function
Standing waves are the complementary case

compositionRules.yields: Complete wave classification: all wave patterns are combinations of traveling and standing components
conditions: When classifying wave behavior in a given system
compositionRules.example: Open-ended pipe: mostly standing waves inside, radiating traveling waves outside

applicabilityPatterns: distinguishing between waves that transport energy and those that oscillate in place
classifying wave behavior in bounded vs unbounded media

primitives.name: Group Delay
primitives.formalStatement: Group delay tau_g = -d(phi)/d(omega) is the rate of change of phase with respect to angular frequency. It measures the time delay experienced by a narrow-band signal passing through a system.
primitives.computationalForm: tau_g = -dphi/d_omega. For a system with transfer function H(omega) = |H|*e^(i*phi(omega)): group delay = -dphi/d_omega. Constant group delay = no distortion.

dependencies.description: Group delay is the time-domain counterpart of group velocity
Group delay relates to phase velocity through dispersion

compositionRules.yields: Complete dispersive analysis: dispersion causes frequency-dependent group delay, spreading pulses
conditions: When analyzing signal distortion in dispersive systems
compositionRules.example: Optical fiber: group delay varies with wavelength, causing pulse broadening over long distances

applicabilityPatterns: measuring how much a system delays signals at different frequencies
analyzing signal distortion in communication systems
